_comment: Chain definition for new-feature-sdlc-skill. Maps directly to its 5-step core workflow.
description: Full feature SDLC chain: inspect repo → backlog → AC → implement → unit/API/E2E tests → review → release

phases.name: Step 2 — Inspect repository and conventions
phases.reason: Inspect tech stack, existing test frameworks, naming conventions, and CI setup before any planning or code

phases.name: Step 3a — Create or update epics and user stories
phases.reason: Translate the approved feature request into structured epics and user stories for the backlog

phases.name: Step 3b — Draft testable acceptance criteria
phases.reason: Convert user stories into testable AC, in Gherkin or TDD format, to guide implementation and test design

phases.name: Step 4 — Implement the feature
phases.reason: Agent implements the minimal production-ready change set using repo conventions from Step 2 and AC from Step 3
phases._note: Agent-handled: the executing model writes the code. Sub-skill dispatched at runtime if stack-specific skill is needed.

phases.name: Step 4 — Unit tests (stack-aware)
phases.reason: Generate and run unit tests for the implemented feature using the repository's native test stack

phases.name: Step 4 — Service and API contract tests
phases.reason: Validate API contract compliance and service behavior if the feature touches API endpoints

phases.name: Step 4 — Frontend and E2E tests
phases.reason: Write and run E2E tests for the UI flow introduced or changed by the feature

phases.name: Step 5 — Verify: review test coverage and quality
phases.reason: Review overall test coverage, automation quality, and evidence completeness across all test layers

phases.name: Step 6 — Close with structured implementation report
phases.reason: Prepare changelog, version bump, and structured implementation report summarising what was done, verified, and left open
phases.query_suffix: STOP BEFORE PUSHING TO GITHUB. Prepare all release artifacts — changelog entry, version bump, release notes, and implementation report — but do NOT run 'git push', 'gh release create', or any command that publishes to the remote. End your output with a clear summary of what is ready and what the human must do to publish.
